title: Statistical Modelling Process
text:
This website contain talks and material given to reseach group of Greenwich University (UK) and to the IWSM, July 2026 in Oslo - Norway.

This is a long version of the IWSM talk. This talk was given to the to the \(M^3\)Impact research group of the University of Greenwich in the beginning of June 2026.
This is the actual IWSM talk to be given at the IWSM on the 3th of July 2026.
This part explains the analysis cfor the ALB data.

title: Statistical Modelling Process
text:
introduction

in the 70’s Box and Jenkins (1970) had a diagram to show what is statistical modelling (SM)
over the last 40 years I have not seen something similar
discuss basic ideas of statistical modelling
using a distributional regression example

title: Statistical Modelling Process
text:
why


The data, are collected to answer the question and this is done through a model.
The concepts are interconnected

title: Statistical Modelling Process
text:
Model


all models are wrong but some are useful.
– George G. E. P. Box (1979)

whether a model is useful depends on the purpose of the study.
A model is useful if it can answer the question

title: Statistical Modelling Process
text:
Regression Models
Breiman (2003)

\[X  {\longrightarrow}  \fbox{NATURE} {\longrightarrow} Y, \]
\[X  {\longrightarrow}  \fbox{Model} {\longrightarrow} Y, \]
\[X  {\longrightarrow} \fbox{G(X)}  {\longrightarrow} Y.\]
the model tries to unmask the unknown function \(G()\) which could be mathematical or algorithmic

title: Statistical Modelling Process
text:
Stochastic model

deterministic model: \(Y =G(X)\)
statistical model: \(Y =G(X, E)\), where \(E\) is the error (a probabilistic component)
\(G(X, E)\) represent both the \(\textit{signal}\) and \(\textit{noise}\) in the data
An additive model assumption: \[Y =G(X)+E\] is a common model assumption for the \(\textit{signal}\) and the \(\textit{noise}\), widely used in machine learning with implicit normal errors.

title: Statistical Modelling Process
text:
Assumptions

an assumption is an axiomatic statement which need to be approximately correct for the model work.
if all the assumptions are approximately correct then the model could be useful
the assumptions often help with the intepretation of a model

title: Statistical Modelling Process
Statistical Modelling Process
text:
Algorithmic <-> Mathematical models

mathematical models, the function \(G(X,E)\) is defined explicitly through the assumptions
algorithmic models make implicit assumptions about the function \(G(X,E)\).
the additive algorithmic model \(G(X)+E\) makes implicit assumptions for \(G(X)\) and the natural error

title: Statistical Modelling Process
text:
Distributional regression models

The parametric Distributional Regression model Stasinopoulos et al. (2024) can be represented as
\[X  {\longrightarrow}  \fbox{$\boldsymbol{\theta}$(X)} {\longrightarrow} D(Y|\boldsymbol{\theta}(X)),\] where \(D(Y|\boldsymbol{\theta}(X))\) estimates the natural error distribution of \(Y\) (conditional on the \(X\)).

title: Statistical Modelling Process
text:
Distributional regression models

The task of parametric distributional Regression is to find ;

which and how the x’s effect the parameters of the distribution i.e \(\boldsymbol{\theta}(X)\) and
the appropriate distribution for the response i.e. \(D(Y|\boldsymbol{\theta})\)

title: Statistical Modelling Process
text:
Distributional regression models

A modern computational approach of the distributional regression, Chai, Fan, and Gu (2026); \[X, U  {\longrightarrow}  \fbox{deep learning} {\longrightarrow} D(Y|X),\] where U represent a simple distribution e.g. uniform which after transformations becomes \(D(Y|X)\) the conditional distribution of \(Y\) given \(X\).

This version of the DR model is NOT easy to interpret because it is a black box.

title: Statistical Modelling Process
text: The Albumin (ALB): (data)

title: Statistical Modelling Process
text:
The Albumin (ALB) fit








Figure 1

title: Statistical Modelling Process
text:
The Albumin (ALB) (question)








Figure 2

title: Statistical Modelling Process
text:
Fitting

either maximize/minimize an objective measure of goodness of fit, \(\mathbb{R}(Y,G(X,E))\), (i.e. a training measure) to obtain the fitted values: \[\widehat{D} = \widehat{G} (X,E)\]
or alternatively simulate from the model \(G(X,E)\) and then average to obtain the fitted values \(\widehat{Y}\) or a fitted distribution \(\widehat{D}\)

title: Statistical Modelling Process
text:
residuals

training residuals: \[\widehat{r}=r(Y, \widehat{D})\] for a suitable function \(r\).
if the model is adequate the training residuals should look as a white noise
white noise, zero mean, constant variance and independent observations
residuals can be used as a diagnostic tool

title: Statistical Modelling Process
text:
Diagnostics

training residuals diagnostics:


are the residuals white noise?

yes: adequate model the model is not under-fitting the data
no: start again

residuals can be calculated for all statistical models, so residual diagnostics is an agnostic tool.


Other types of diagnostics could check other assumptions or possibly focus in answering the question.

title: Statistical Modelling Process
text: Classical statistical modelling

title: Statistical Modelling Process
text: Classical statistical modelling 1

title: Statistical Modelling Process
text:
Classical statistical modelling: comments

the classical statistical modelling approach can find an adequate model
but can not check for over-fitting
we overfit if the model is close to the data but it does not generalised on new data
data partition can help with this

title: Statistical Modelling Process
text:
Data Partition

data partition helps to check over-fitting and to improve inference

title: Statistical Modelling Process
text:
types of Partition

single partition (holdout samples)
multiple partitions (bootstrapping, K-fold cross validation )

importantly the data are divided to

training data
test data

title: Statistical Modelling Process
text:
stack of models

for each data set there could be several models which are not under-fitting or over-fitting the data called the Rashomon set
several models from the Rashomon set are interpretable Rudin et al. (2024).
how to chose between different adequate models?

use a test measure of goodness of fit or even better use a focus test measure of goodness of fit
or use model average

title: Statistical Modelling Process
Statistical Modelling Process
text:
Model comparison

use a test measure of goodness of fit
for the ALB data sets we use

deviance (\(-2 \ell\)) as global test measure
\(\chi^2\) values as focused test measure


Both applied to the ALB data and lead to the BCTo distribution as the best distribution.

title: Statistical Modelling Process
text:
ALB diagnostics








Figure 3

title: Statistical Modelling Process
text:
ALB diagnostics 2








Figure 4

title: Statistical Modelling Process
text: Modern Statistical Modelling

title: Statistical Modelling Process
text:
Discussion

most problems in SM arise if the assumptions are not correct
measures of goodness of fit are needed for both model fitting and model comparison
the two measures of goodness of fit can be different
ideally measures of goodness of fit should focus on the question
the measure of goodness of fit can be model agnostic
diagnostics evaluated at test and training data should be part of any SM
interpretable (mostly mathematical) models easier to check.
“Stop explaining black box machine learning models for high stakes decisions and use interpretable models instead” (Rudin 2019)

title: Statistical Modelling Process
text:
end






   see also the R package gamlss2

text:

title: ALB analysis
text:
The ALB are in the latest version 6.0-10 of the gamlss.data package. (Note that we may have to get the latest version from the gamlss universe.
Getting the data from gamlss.data;

library(gamlss)
data(ALB)
dim(ALB)

[1] 3429    2

head(ALB)

   ALB   ga
1 42.1 12.1
2 42.4 11.6
3 43.8 12.7
4 40.3 12.5
5 44.0 12.0
6 44.4 12.9


Now we plot the data;

plot(ALB~ga, data=ALB)








The data were collected when healthy pregnant woman had their scans at Kings College hospital. The scan are taken routinely four times. That explains why they are caps in data for different gestational age. Notice that ate this data they are NOT repeated observations, all 3429 women are different.






Purpose



The purpose of the study is to try to identify patients with low ALB

title: ALB analysis
text:
The ALB are in the latest version 6.0-10 of the gamlss.data package. (Note that we may have to get the latest version from the gamlss universe.
Getting the data from gamlss.data;

library(gamlss)
data(ALB)
dim(ALB)

[1] 3429    2

head(ALB)

   ALB   ga
1 42.1 12.1
2 42.4 11.6
3 43.8 12.7
4 40.3 12.5
5 44.0 12.0
6 44.4 12.9


Now we plot the data;

plot(ALB~ga, data=ALB)








The data were collected when healthy pregnant woman had their scans at Kings College hospital. The scan are taken routinely four times. That explains why they are caps in data for different gestational age. Notice that ate this data they are NOT repeated observations, all 3429 women are different.






Purpose



The purpose of the study is to try to identify patients with low ALB

title: ALB analysis
text:
Fit a model
Here we are using a distributional regression model, GAMLSS, to answer the question.In a distributional model all the parameters of the distributions are functions of the explanatory variable gestation age, ga.
Any GAMLSS regression model needs two type of assumtions to be fitted:

the assumed distribution for the response i.e family=GA,
the selection of covariates; In fact in general since we are modelling all distribution parameters as functions of the covariates, we are looking which covariate affect which distribution parameter.

To start a selection procedure in GAMLSS we need a starting model.
Here we fit two starting models;

a normal model e.g. family=NO which works as a staring model for the function chooseDist()


m00 <- gamlss(   ALB ~ pb(ga),
    sigma.formula = ~ pb(ga),
      nu.formula = ~ pb(ga),  
     tau.formula = ~ pb(ga),
             family = NO, data = ALB) 

GAMLSS-RS iteration 1: Global Deviance = 15306.02 
GAMLSS-RS iteration 2: Global Deviance = 15306.09 
GAMLSS-RS iteration 3: Global Deviance = 15306.09 



a BCTo model e.g. family=BCTo which  is used as staring model for the functionchooseDistCV()(see note below for an explanation why we have to do that). The functionchooseDistCV()`is a new function and has to be sourced.


m0 <- gamlss(   ALB ~ pb(ga),
    sigma.formula = ~ pb(ga),
      nu.formula = ~ pb(ga),  
     tau.formula = ~ pb(ga),
             family = BCTo, data = ALB, n.cyc=100) 

GAMLSS-RS iteration 1: Global Deviance = 15198.53 
GAMLSS-RS iteration 2: Global Deviance = 15168.6 
GAMLSS-RS iteration 3: Global Deviance = 15167.18 
GAMLSS-RS iteration 4: Global Deviance = 15164.12 
GAMLSS-RS iteration 5: Global Deviance = 15160.44 
GAMLSS-RS iteration 6: Global Deviance = 15157.94 
GAMLSS-RS iteration 7: Global Deviance = 15156.61 
GAMLSS-RS iteration 8: Global Deviance = 15155.96 
GAMLSS-RS iteration 9: Global Deviance = 15155.65 
GAMLSS-RS iteration 10: Global Deviance = 15155.52 
GAMLSS-RS iteration 11: Global Deviance = 15155.45 
GAMLSS-RS iteration 12: Global Deviance = 15155.42 
GAMLSS-RS iteration 13: Global Deviance = 15155.4 
GAMLSS-RS iteration 14: Global Deviance = 15155.39 
GAMLSS-RS iteration 15: Global Deviance = 15155.38 
GAMLSS-RS iteration 16: Global Deviance = 15155.38 
GAMLSS-RS iteration 17: Global Deviance = 15155.38 
GAMLSS-RS iteration 18: Global Deviance = 15155.38 








Note



The nu.formula = ~ pb(ga) and tau.formula = ~ pb(ga) will be not be used by chooseDistCV() unless more that two parameter are involved in the distribution. This has to be investigated further.








reminder



I have to find out why the function chooseDistCV() do not accepts the normal error model like chooseDist().

title: ALB analysis
text:
Finding a Distributions (training data)
The typical function for finding a distribution is chooseDist(). The problem is that the function only finds suitable distributions in the training data using GAIC as the training measure of goodness of fit and therefor it do not tes for over-fitting

D1=chooseDist(m00, data=ALB, type="realplus", parallel="snow", ncpus = 10)

minimum GAIC(k= 2 ) family: BCT 
minimum GAIC(k= 3.84 ) family: BCT 
minimum GAIC(k= 8.14 ) family: BCPEo 


For penalties \(k=2\) and \(k=2.84\) the BCT is coming best while for BIC the BCPEo
Here we print a table of the GAIC for all fitted distributions;

library(knitr)
kable(D1)

The problem with the above analysis is that the measure of goodness of fit, GAIC, was validated on the training data only This show that the model is probably adequate but it do not check for overfitting.

title: ALB analysis
text:
Finding a Distributions (cross validation)
Next we use a \(10^{th}\) fold cross validation for choosing the distribution. Cross validation hopefully prevent overfiting.
There are two criteria we used here;

the test deviance which is a global criterion, that is, it evaluates how the distribution fits overall.
a chi-square criterion which focus on the lower centiles of the distribution.

The new function chooseDistCV() is used which is not in the gamlss package (yet) so we have to source it;

source("chooseDistCVParallel.R")


test deviance
First we use a global measurement of goodness of fit criterion the test deviance. That is -2 the log-Likelihood evaluated at the test data rather that the training ones. I order to do that we have to define the function measure_of_goodness_of_fit() which chooseDistcCV() uses to evaluate the different distributions. Here we define the function;

measure_of_goodness_of_fit <- function(object)
{
  object$CV
}

Now to avoid fitting all possible positive range distributions using cross validation we speed the process by using only a selected set of 5 distributions. The gamma., GA and the log-normal, LOGNO, distributions both very popular in the statistical literature and the distributions BCTo, BCPEo and BCCGo popular in centile estimation (associated with the LMS method). The R code is as follows;

PP = chooseDistCV(m0, data=ALB, type="extra", 
          extra=c("GA", "LOGNO", "BCCGo", "BCPEo", "BCTo"))

PP

    BCTo    BCPEo    BCCGo       GA    LOGNO 
15204.66 15207.80 15240.09 15388.38 15429.09 


The simple use of the chooseDistcCV() function results to output which prints the fold number after a new model is fitted to each partitio. One could avoid the unnecessary output by using parallel processing.

set.seed(123)
PP1 = chooseDistCV(m0, data=ALB, type="extra", 
          extra=c("GA", "LOGNO", "BCCGo", "BCPEo", "BCTo"),
          parallel="snow", ncpus = 10)


PP1

    BCTo    BCPEo    BCCGo       GA    LOGNO 
15212.52 15215.71 15250.28 15390.83 15432.00 








Note



There are few things to note here;

Any cross validation will have results depending on the original computer seed
Using parallel computation the original seed do not apply in all CPUS therefore the results are in general different.




Fortunately the BCTo distribution gives the best fit in both runs. This is not always the case.






checking the code



The code below is used to find out whether using the factor which creates the partition using the argument rand to generate the partitions makes any diffence. It looks that it does not.



set.seed(123)
sam <- sample(10 , dim(ALB)[1], replace=TRUE)
PP <- chooseDistCV(m0, data=ALB, type="extra",
          extra=c("GA", "LOGNO", "BCCGo", "BCPEo", "BCTo"), rand=sam)

It looks that the the option rand=sam is NOT working properly in the chooseDistCV()




chi-squared (the focus measure )
First bring the functions in;

source("Chi2.R")

Define the focus measure of goodness of fit;

measure_of_goodness_of_fit <- function(object)
 {
   cal_chi2(object$residCV, xvar=ALB$ga, breaks = c(0,17,25,34.5, 50), tail.percent = 5,  side= "lower")
 }

Now choose distribution using CV with no parallel computaions.

 PPP = chooseDistCV(m0, data=ALB, type="extra",
           extra=c("GA", "LOGNO", "BCCGo", "BCPEo", "BCTo"))

title: ALB analysis
section: Finding a Distributions (using the training data)
text:
Finding a Distributions (using the training data)
The typical function for finding a distribution is chooseDist(). The problem is that the function only finds suitable distributions for the training data using an Generalized AKaike information criterion, GAIC, as the training  measure of goodness of fit. Therefor it do not check for over-fitting.

D1=chooseDist(m00, data=ALB, type="realplus", parallel="snow", ncpus = 10)

minimum GAIC(k= 2 ) family: BCT 
minimum GAIC(k= 3.84 ) family: BCT 
minimum GAIC(k= 8.14 ) family: BCPEo 


With penalties \(k=2\) and \(k=2.84\) the BCT is coming best while for BIC the BCPEo.
Next we print a table of all the fitted GAIC;

kable(D1)

The problem with the above analysis is that the measure of goodness of fit, GAIC, was validated on the training data only This show that the model is probably adequate but it do not check for overfitting.
